citation: Fick, S.E. & Hijmans, R.J. (2017). WorldClim 2: new 1km spatial resolution climate surfaces for global land areas. International Journal of Climatology, 37(12), 4302–4315.
preprocessing_notes: Resample to 1 km COG, clip to AOI. BIO1=Annual Mean Temp; BIO12=Annual Precipitation; BIO15=Precipitation Seasonality most relevant for agroforestry suitability.
limitations: 30-year climatological mean; does not capture interannual variability or extremes. Resolution may mask local topographic effects. Not for: Site-level microclimate analysis or real-time weather monitoring
description: Global gridded bioclimatic variables (BIO1-BIO19) derived from 1970-2000 monthly temperature and precipitation.

citation: Karger, D.N. et al. (2017). Climatologies at high resolution for the earth's land surface areas (CHELSA). Scientific Data, 4, 170122.
preprocessing_notes: Resample to 1 km COG. CHELSA-BIOCLIM+ Version 1.2 used by Yadav et al. (2024) for agroforestry suitability in Punjab. Use Bio1 (Annual Mean Temp), Bio12 (Annual Precipitation), Tmax_Jan, Tmax_May.
limitations: Statistical downscaling from ERA5; may show artefacts in complex terrain. Less validated over Africa and Central Asia. Not for: Short-term weather forecasting or sub-field precision agriculture
description: Bias-corrected high-resolution global bioclimatic variables, climatology 1981-2010 (~1 km).

citation: Karger, D.N. et al. (2023). CHELSA-W5E5: Daily 1 km meteorological forcing data for climate impact studies. Earth System Science Data, 15, 2445–2464.
preprocessing_notes: Use delta-change approach relative to baseline. Clip to project AOI. Ensemble mean of available CMIP6 GCMs preferred.
limitations: Ensemble spread is wide in tropical regions. Precipitation projections carry higher uncertainty than temperature. Not for: Single-GCM deterministic planning; local infrastructure design
description: CHELSA bioclimatic variables under SSP2-4.5 around 2050 (CMIP6 ensemble downscaled).

citation: Vicente-Serrano, S.M. et al. (2010). A Multiscalar Drought Index Sensitive to Global Warming: The Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index. Journal of Climate, 23(7), 1696–1718.
preprocessing_notes: Extract SPEI-12 (12-month accumulation window) for drought risk. Compute mean and trend over 1981–2010 baseline. Bilinearly resample to 1 km for overlay.
limitations: Very coarse native resolution (0.5°). Misses sub-regional drought heterogeneity. PET estimation method affects values. Not for: Farm-level drought monitoring; flash drought detection
description: Standardized Precipitation-Evapotranspiration Index, global ~55 km, monthly 1901-present.

citation: Poggio, L. et al. (2021). SoilGrids 2.0: producing soil information for the globe with quantified spatial uncertainty. SOIL, 7, 217–240.
preprocessing_notes: Extract layers at 0–30 cm depth (topsoil) and 30–60 cm (subsoil). Resample to 1 km. pH, SOC, clay content, CEC are primary agroforestry suitability constraints per Ahmad et al. (2020), Chuma et al. (2021), Haile (2024).
limitations: Prediction uncertainty is high in data-sparse regions (much of sub-Saharan Africa). Point observations biased toward accessible areas. Temporal static — does not capture land-use change effects on soil. Not for: Precision soil management at plot level; nutrient management plans without ground-truthing
description: Global digital soil mapping (250 m) of physical and chemical soil properties at six depths.

citation: FAO/IIASA/ISRIC/ISSCAS/JRC (2023). Harmonized World Soil Database version 2.0. FAO, Rome, Italy & IIASA, Laxenburg, Austria.
preprocessing_notes: Used by Ahmad et al. (2020) as primary soil input for South Asia agroforestry suitability. Join soil mapping unit attributes on raster values. Extract topsoil properties for suitability scoring.
limitations: Coarser conceptual resolution than SoilGrids despite same pixel size — many large mapping units contain internally variable soils. Outdated in rapidly changing agricultural areas. Not for: Detailed site characterisation; soil carbon accounting
description: Harmonized World Soil Database v2 — global ~1 km soil units with property attributes.

citation: Farr, T.G. et al. (2007). The Shuttle Radar Topography Mission. Reviews of Geophysics, 45(2), RG2004.
preprocessing_notes: Derive slope (degrees) and aspect from DEM in GEE. Slope is primary biophysical constraint for agroforestry machinery access and erosion risk. Used across nearly all 23 AF papers in stocktake (Ahmad et al. 2017, 2018, 2020; Chuma et al. 2021; Haile 2024; Nath et al. 2021; Mushtaq et al. 2023; Seja et al. 2022; Wotlolan et al. 2021).
limitations: Voids in high-relief areas filled with ASTER or other interpolation methods — check for artefacts. Vegetation canopy not filtered — potential overestimation of elevation under forest cover. Not for: Hydraulic modelling requiring bare-earth DEM; landslide hazard at plot scale
description: Shuttle Radar Topography Mission digital elevation model, 3 arc-second (~90 m), 56°S-60°N.

citation: Zanaga, D. et al. (2022). ESA WorldCover 10m 2021 v200. Zenodo.
preprocessing_notes: Reclassify to agricultural mask (cropland, grassland, sparse vegetation) to define eligible agroforestry conversion area. Exclude built-up, water bodies, bare soil with high salinity. Aggregate to 100m or 1km for pipeline. Used as LULC base by multiple AF studies.
limitations: Mixed-pixel confusion in fragmented agricultural landscapes. Annual snapshot — does not capture seasonal cultivation. Accuracy lower in arid landscapes and under cloud cover. Not for: Crop-type mapping; sub-field land use classification
description: ESA WorldCover 10 m global land-cover map (2021), Sentinel-1/2 derived 11-class classification.

dataset_name: Global Aridity Index and PET v3 (CGIAR-CSI)
citation: Trabucco, A. & Zomer, R. (2019). Global Aridity Index and Potential Evapotranspiration (ET0) Climate Database v2. CGIAR-CSI GeoPortal.
preprocessing_notes: AI < 0.5 = dryland; used to mask unsuitable extreme arid zones for agroforestry. Ahmad et al. (2020) use aridity index as key suitability layer for South Asia. Nath et al. (2021) use Global Aridity Index for Himalayan region.
limitations: Derived from WorldClim 1970–2000 climatology — does not reflect recent drying trends. PET estimated with Penman-Monteith assuming reference crop. Not for: Real-time drought monitoring; current water stress assessment
description: Global-Aridity Index v3 — long-term P/PET ratio at ~1 km (CGIAR-CSI).

citation: UNEP-WCMC & IUCN (2024). Protected Planet: The World Database on Protected Areas (WDPA). Cambridge: UNEP-WCMC and IUCN.
preprocessing_notes: Rasterize to 100m. Hard exclusion mask for IUCN categories Ia, Ib, II for agroforestry suitability (conversion not permitted). Categories V, VI may allow agroforestry within management plans — flag as conditional. Used by Chuma et al. (2021) as exclusion layer around Itombwe Nature Reserve.
limitations: Boundary data quality varies by country. Some protected areas are paper parks with little enforcement. Does not capture community conserved areas (OECMs) comprehensively. Not for: Legal compliance decisions; site-level permitting
description: World Database on Protected Areas — global polygons of legally protected sites (UNEP-WCMC / IUCN).

citation: OpenStreetMap contributors (2024). OpenStreetMap. www.openstreetmap.org.
preprocessing_notes: Compute Euclidean distance to nearest road of any class. Used by Chuma et al. (2021) as infrastructure accessibility layer. Apply 5km threshold as moderate accessibility constraint for agroforestry market access. is_scenario_candidate = true (road investment can change this).
limitations: OSM coverage quality is highly variable in rural sub-Saharan Africa, Central Asia, and remote tropical regions — significant under-mapping in key project areas. Validate against national road datasets where possible. Not for: Routing or logistics planning; travel-time modelling without validation
description: OpenStreetMap global road network vector lines, multiple admin levels.

citation: WorldPop (2020). Global High Resolution Population Denominators Project - Funded by The Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation (OPP1134076). University of Southampton.
preprocessing_notes: Aggregate to 1 km for pipeline. Use as proxy for population exposure in climate risk and as denominator for opportunity space poverty metrics.
limitations: Constrained model relies on settlement/building footprint mapping — may undercount nomadic/dispersed rural populations. Accuracy varies with census quality of input data. Not for: Individual household targeting; electoral boundary analysis
description: Global gridded population estimates at 100 m for the year stated (WorldPop).

dataset_name: FAO Global Soil Organic Carbon Map (GSOCmap) v1.6.1
citation: FAO & ITPS (2018). Global Soil Organic Carbon Map (GSOCmap) v1.6.1. Technical Report. FAO, Rome.
preprocessing_notes: Use as carbon co-benefit layer in opportunity space. Mendonça et al. (2023) use SOC sequestration potential as an agroforestry prioritisation criterion. Kiziridis et al. (2026) map silvopastoral carbon sequestration. Complement with FAO GSOCseq for sequestration rate.
limitations: Country submissions vary in quality and methodology. National and sub-national SOC maps (where available) should be used in preference. Not for: Carbon crediting baselines without country-specific validation; REDD+ baseline setting
description: FAO Global Soil Organic Carbon map — country-validated 1 km topsoil (0-30 cm) SOC stocks.

dataset_name: FAO-IIASA Global Agro-Ecological Zones v4 (33-class AEZ)
citation: FAO & IIASA (2021). Global Agro-Ecological Zones – GAEZ v4. FAO, Rome & IIASA, Laxenburg.
preprocessing_notes: Use for AEZ context classification in T7. Key AEZ groups for agroforestry context overrides: humid tropics, sub-humid tropics, semi-arid, highland tropics.
limitations: AEZ boundaries are climate-envelope based and do not capture soil or management diversity within zones. Not for: Crop yield modelling at field scale
description: Global Agro-Ecological Zones v4 — 33-class agro-ecological zoning at ~1 km.

dataset_name: FAO/IFAD Farming Systems of the World (Dixon et al. 2001 / updated)
citation: Dixon, J., Gulliver, A. & Gibbon, D. (2001). Farming Systems and Poverty: Improving Farmers' Livelihoods in a Changing World. FAO and World Bank, Rome & Washington D.C.
preprocessing_notes: Rasterize at 10 km then resample to 1 km with majority resampling. Used to populate T7 farming system contexts and to apply context-specific suitability overrides in T4.
limitations: Classification is 20+ years old; agricultural systems have shifted significantly in some regions. Mixed-farming boundaries are expert-delineated and coarse. Not for: Subnational farming system analysis without local validation
description: FAO Dixon farming-systems typology for developing regions (Africa/Asia/Latin America).

dataset_name: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform — Subnational Poverty Estimates
citation: World Bank (2024). Poverty and Inequality Platform (PIP). World Bank Group, Washington DC.
preprocessing_notes: Interpolate to raster using admin boundary weights and WorldPop population density. Combine with spatial poverty maps (e.g. CGIAR CELL5M poverty layers) for higher-resolution estimates.
limitations: Sub-national resolution limited to admin-1 or -2 in most countries. Survey years vary. Does not capture within-admin spatial variation. Not for: Household targeting; beneficiary selection for project implementation
description: World Bank PIP subnational poverty headcount estimates at admin-1 (admin-2 where available).

citation: Hansen, M.C. et al. (2013). High-Resolution Global Maps of 21st-Century Forest Cover Change. Science, 342(6160), 850–853.
preprocessing_notes: Current tree cover = baseline (2000) − loss + gain. Used to identify areas below 10% tree cover as agroforestry expansion opportunity and areas with existing tree cover for context. Singh et al. (2024) and Ahmad et al. (2020) use Hansen tree cover as AF suitability input.
limitations: Confusion between natural forest and plantation/agroforestry systems. Understory trees in agroforestry are often missed at 30m resolution. Not for: Legal deforestation monitoring for jurisdictional REDD+; plot-level timber volume
description: Global Forest Change tree cover and loss layers (Hansen et al.) at 30 m.

citation: Fick, S.E. & Hijmans, R.J. (2017). WorldClim 2: new 1km spatial resolution climate surfaces for global land areas. International Journal of Climatology, 37(12), 4302-4315.
preprocessing_notes: Rainfall is the second most frequent input variable (38 of 85 papers). Extract mean annual precipitation and wet-season monthly totals. Use for SCS-CN runoff depth calculation combined with HSG soil map. Buraihi & Shariff (2015) use projected precipitation to assess future RWH viability under climate change.
limitations: 30-year climatological mean; interannual variability not captured. Resolution too coarse for localised orographic precipitation in complex terrain. Not for: Flood frequency analysis; design-storm estimation for dam sizing
description: Climate Hazards Group InfraRed Precipitation with Stations, ~5 km daily precipitation 1981-present.

dataset_name: HydroSHEDS Watershed Boundaries and River Network
citation: Lehner, B. et al. (2008). New global hydrography derived from spaceborne elevation data. Eos, Transactions AGU, 89(10), 93-94.
preprocessing_notes: Use HydroBASINS level 8-10 for sub-watershed delineation context. Stream order used as a primary RWH site-type discriminator: first/second-order streams suit check dams and gully plugs; third/fourth-order streams suit percolation tanks; larger streams suit small dams. Kumar et al. (2018) and Singh et al. (2016) use stream order as a primary MCDA criterion.
limitations: Derived from 3-arc-second SRTM; misses ephemeral channels in flat arid terrain. Resolution insufficient for first-order gully mapping. Not for: Detailed gully morphometry; flash flood routing
description: Composite SCS curve-number runoff potential surface combining land-cover and hydrologic soil group.

citation: Farr et al. (2007). SRTM. Reviews of Geophysics.
preprocessing_notes: WH module alias for SRTM. See srtm_dem_90m entry for full notes.
limitations: Voids in high-relief terrain. Not for: Engineering design
description: Shuttle Radar Topography Mission digital elevation model, 1 arc-second (~30 m), 56°S-60°N.

citation: Fick & Hijmans (2017). WorldClim 2. International Journal of Climatology.
preprocessing_notes: Extract mean annual precipitation and wet-season monthly totals for SCS-CN runoff calculation.
limitations: 30-year climatological mean; interannual variability not captured. Not for: Flood frequency analysis; design-storm estimation
description: WorldClim v2 monthly precipitation (1970-2000) at ~1 km.

citation: IFPRI (2024) MapSPAM 2020 Global Spatially-Disaggregated Crop Production.
preprocessing_notes: Use harvested-area fraction as graded crop-presence constraint.
limitations: ~10 km; allocation-model estimate, not observed. Coarse for sub-national targeting. Not for: plot siting.
description: MapSPAM 2020 — gridded crop production allocation for cocoa at ~10 km.

citation: GADM (2022) Database of Global Administrative Areas v4.1.
preprocessing_notes: Country/region polygons for AOI + admin context scoping.
limitations: Boundary vintages vary by country; non-commercial licence. Not for: dispute adjudication.
description: GADM Database of Global Administrative Areas v4.1 — admin-0/1/2/3 boundary polygons.
Potapov et al. (2022) global cropland extent for the year 2019 at 30m, derived from Landsat time series. Binary cropland mask.
citation: Potapov, P., Turubanova, S., Hansen, M. C., Tyukavina, A., Zalles, V., Khan, A., Song, X.-P., Pickens, A., Shen, Q., & Cortez, J. (2022). Global maps of cropland extent and change show accelerated cropland expansion in the twenty-first century. Nature Food, 3, 19-28.
preprocessing_notes: Use as binary cropland presence layer.
limitations: Cropland definition includes fallow + temporary cropping; rotational use may misclassify.

description: ESA WorldCereal product (2020-2021 season) at 10m: active cropland + 4 seasonal crop classes (temporary crops, irrigated/rainfed). Used here as cropland + irrigation auxiliary.
citation: Van Tricht, K., Degerickx, J., Gilliams, S., Zanaga, D., Battude, M., Grosu, A., Brombacher, J., Pelayo, Y. M., Lesiv, M., Buchhorn, M., Smets, B., Tarko, A., Szantoi, Z., Bauwens, I., Defourny, P., & Szilagyi, I. (2023). WorldCereal: a dynamic open-source system for global-scale, seasonal, and reproducible crop and irrigation mapping. Earth System Science Data, 15, 5491-5515.
preprocessing_notes: Multi-layer: active_cropland, temporary_crops, irrigation. Pick layer per use.
limitations: Calibration variable by region; irrigation class is presence not intensity.

dataset_name: Gridded Livestock of the World v4 (GLW4, 2020)
description: FAO/ILRI/UMR-SELMET gridded livestock density (per species: cattle, buffalo, sheep, goats, pigs, chickens, ducks, horses) at ~10 km, year 2015 baseline (v4 release 2024).
citation: Gilbert, M., Nicolas, G., Cinardi, G., Van Boeckel, T. P., Vanwambeke, S. O., Wint, G. R. W., & Robinson, T. P. (2018). Global distribution data for cattle, buffaloes, horses, sheep, goats, pigs, chickens and ducks in 2010. Scientific Data, 5, 180227. (v4 update 2024).
preprocessing_notes: Sum across ruminant species (cattle + buffalo + sheep + goats) for total ruminant density; convert TLU using ILRI weights for fair comparison.
limitations: Modelled (dasymetric), not observed; uncertainty highest in pastoral / mobile-herd contexts.

dataset_name: FAO Global Map of Irrigation Areas v5 (GMIA)
description: FAO/Rheinische Friedrich-Wilhelms-Universität Bonn GMIA v5 — area equipped for irrigation as % of cell, at 5 arc-minute (~10 km).
citation: Siebert, S., Henrich, V., Frenken, K., & Burke, J. (2013). Update of the digital global map of irrigation areas to version 5. FAO/Rheinische Friedrich-Wilhelms-Universität Bonn.
preprocessing_notes: Area equipped for irrigation is potential, not actual irrigation use; combine with WorldCereal irrigation for refinement.
limitations: Coarse resolution; lags real-world updates; rainfed mosaics may be misclassified at coarse aggregation.

description: Global Biodiversity Intactness Index — modelled remaining proportion of local biodiversity intactness vs an undisturbed baseline, ~1 km global gridded surface.
citation: Newbold, T., Hudson, L. N., Arnell, A. P., Contu, S., De Palma, A., Ferrier, S., ... & Purvis, A. (2016). Has land use pushed terrestrial biodiversity beyond the planetary boundary? A global assessment. Science, 353(6296), 288-291.
preprocessing_notes: BII is a 0-100 (or 0-1) intactness index — invert if framing biodiversity_priority as 'higher = more concern' (i.e. priority = 1 - intactness, so lowest-intactness areas surface).
limitations: Modelled (PREDICTS database); systematic uncertainty in under-sampled biomes. Doesn't capture rare-species hotspots — pair with KBA polygons as a binary high-priority flag.
